era_context: Late 15th century: Portuguese maritime expansion accelerating; Ottoman consolidation in the eastern Mediterranean; Italian Renaissance flourishing; Ming China under the Hongwu and Yongle successors; Aztec Empire under Tizoc; the Iberian Reconquista in its final phase; England transitioning from the Wars of the Roses to Tudor rule.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written documentation; indigenous records sparse)
Interior Southeast Asia (limited European contact; local chronicles underrepresented)

events.title: Henry VII defeats Richard III at the Battle of Bosworth Field; Tudor dynasty established
events.description: Henry Tudor defeated King Richard III at the Battle of Bosworth Field on August 22, 1485, effectively ending the Wars of the Roses and establishing the Tudor dynasty. Richard III was killed in the battle, and Henry Tudor was crowned king as Henry VII. Henry VII's victory marked a significant turning point in English history, ending decades of civil conflict and establishing a new dynasty that would rule England for over a century. Henry VII worked to consolidate royal authority, strengthen the economy, and establish England's position in European politics.

sources.name: Contemporary English chronicles (e.g., Polydore Vergil, Historia Anglica)

events.certainty_note: The battle and its outcome are well-documented in English historical records and contemporary sources.
cross_references: 1487-001 (Battle of Stoke Field, Henry VII's consolidation)

events.title: Ottoman-Mamluk War begins
events.description: The Ottoman-Mamluk War (1485–1491) began as tensions between the Ottoman Empire and the Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt escalated into open conflict. The war was fought over control of trade routes, territorial influence, and regional hegemony in the eastern Mediterranean and the Levant. The conflict resulted in Ottoman victory and the incorporation of the Levant and Egypt into the Ottoman Empire, marking a significant shift in the balance of power in the eastern Mediterranean and the Middle East.

sources.name: Ottoman and Mamluk chronicles and diplomatic records
Historiographic consensus (multiple modern histories of the Ottoman and Mamluk empires)

events.certainty_note: The Ottoman-Mamluk War is well-documented; the specific dating of its beginning to 1485 or 1486 varies slightly among sources.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet continues operations in the Indian Ocean
events.description: Admiral Zheng He continued his series of massive maritime expeditions (the Treasure Voyages) under the Ming Dynasty, exploring and establishing tributary relationships across the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and Southeast Asia. By 1485, Zheng He's expeditions had already fundamentally mapped the Indian Ocean and established Chinese diplomatic and trade networks across Asia and Africa. These voyages represented the most advanced naval technology and organization of the era.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Ming Dynasty patron, deceased 1424; continued under successors)

sources.name: Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan (The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)

events.certainty_note: The broader Treasure Voyage program is well-documented; specific activities in 1485 are less precisely dated but consistent with the documented expedition schedule.

events.title: Tizoc dies; end of his reign as Aztec Emperor
events.description: Tizoc, the Aztec Emperor, died in 1485 after a relatively short and unsuccessful reign (r. 1481–1485). Tizoc was known for his military failures and his unpopularity among the Aztec nobility. His death marked the end of a period of relative weakness for the Aztec Empire and paved the way for the accession of his successor, Ahuitzotl, who would prove to be a far more capable and aggressive ruler. Tizoc's reign is sometimes characterized as a low point in Aztec power before the empire's resurgence under Ahuitzotl.

sources.name: Codex Mendoza (compiled post-conquest but based on pre-Columbian sources)
Sahagún, Florentine Codex (16th-century compilation of Nahua accounts)

events.certainty_note: Tizoc's death is attested in post-conquest sources; the specific dating to 1485 is based on historiographic consensus, though some sources place it in 1486.

events.title: Portuguese exploration of the African coast continues under John II
events.description: King John II of Portugal continued his systematic patronage of maritime exploration along the African coast. Portuguese explorers, including Diogo Cão and others, were mapping the African coastline and establishing trading posts and diplomatic contacts with local rulers. These expeditions were part of Portugal's broader strategy to find a sea route to India and to establish a monopoly on African trade. By 1485, Portuguese exploration had reached the southern regions of West-Central Africa.

sources.name: Portuguese royal records and navigational accounts
Later Portuguese chronicles and historiographic accounts

events.certainty_note: The broader Portuguese exploration program is well-documented; specific events in 1485 are less precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Bosworth Field is sometimes portrayed as a clear-cut victory that definitively ended the Wars of the Roses; modern scholarship emphasizes that Henry VII's position remained somewhat precarious for several more years, and that the battle was significant but not conclusive. The dating of Tizoc's death is uncertain; some sources place it in 1486 rather than 1485. The significance of the Ottoman-Mamluk War is sometimes overstated as a sudden shift in power; modern scholarship emphasizes the gradual nature of Ottoman expansion and the complexity of the political situation.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1485 is rich for Europe (particularly England, Portugal, and the Ottoman Empire) due to extensive diplomatic archives, royal records, and contemporary chronicles. The Ottoman and Mamluk sources are also well-preserved. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history is less well-attested. Sub-Saharan Africa, interior Southeast Asia, and the Pacific remain poorly documented for this year. Mesoamerican history relies heavily on post-conquest compilations and codices, which introduce chronological uncertainty. The Battle of Bosworth Field has been extensively studied and is well-documented, but some details remain contested among historians.

graph_edges.note: Henry VII's victory at Bosworth Field led to his consolidation of power, including the defeat of Lambert Simnel at Stoke Field
Henry VII's establishment of the Tudor dynasty enabled his foreign policy initiatives, including the Treaty of Medina del Campo
Tizoc's death led directly to Ahuitzotl's accession and the resurgence of Aztec power
The Ottoman-Mamluk War (1485–1491) resulted in Ottoman victory and expansion
